one_line_takeaway: Because nearly all modern AIGC-forgery detectors reuse the same public CLIP backbone, an attacker with only white-box access to that shared backbone (no access to any specific detector's classifier head) can use text-anchored, source-aware multi-modal guidance to erase forgery signals in the shared feature space, collapsing detection accuracy to near zero across six state-of-the-art detectors and even corrupting their generated textual explanations.
key_finding: At epsilon=8/255, ForgeryEraser drives Fake-image detection accuracy to single digits for most models: LEGION 74.7% clean to 0.5% (R-delta -99.4%), Forensics Adapter 62.5% to 5.6% (R-delta -91.0%), Effort 90.8% to 9.4% (R-delta -89.7%), AIDE 96.5% to 14.2% (R-delta -85.2%), FakeVLM 99.3% to 37.6% (R-delta -62.2%), SIDA(Tampered) 93.7% to 10.0% (R-delta -89.3%), SIDA(Full-Synthesis) 99.5% to 26.5% (R-delta -73.0%). Even at the low budget epsilon=4/255, SIDA's accuracy on Local Editing (Tampered) drops over 87% relatively. Real-image accuracy is often unaffected or even improves under attack ('Semantic Refinement'), most notably Effort's Real-image accuracy rising from 67.2% (clean) to 95.5% (epsilon=8/255). Cross-generator generalization on LEGION/UFD shows Fake accuracy collapsing to near zero across both Diffusion generators (avg 81.9% clean to 0.25% adversarial) and GAN generators (avg 91.6% clean to 0.76% adversarial). Ablation (Table 3) shows a monotonic ranking: Baseline > ForgeryEraser (attacks best) < Coarse-grained < Untargeted (attacks worst); e.g., on LEGION Fake accuracy: Baseline 74.7%, Untargeted 34.5%, Coarse-grained 3.5%, ForgeryEraser 0.5%. Source-aware anchor matching outperforms mismatched anchors, confirming the value of the domain-specific anchor design. Robustness experiments show the attack retains substantial R-delta impact under JPEG compression down to Q=50 and under Gaussian blur (except a confounded Effort case attributed to the victim's own baseline sensitivity to blur, not attack failure). Qualitatively, SIDA and FakeVLM's generated textual explanations flip polarity post-attack (e.g., 'physically incorrect reflections' becomes 'physically accurate light interactions'; 'lifeless eyes' becomes 'natural moisture gradients'), demonstrating the attack corrupts not just verdicts but generated forensic reasoning.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is ForgeryEraser, a black-box (relative to the downstream detector) universal anti-forensics attack against AIGC/image-forgery detectors. It exploits the fact that most state-of-the-art detectors reuse a shared, publicly accessible upstream VLM backbone (CLIP or OpenCLIP) as a frozen feature extractor. The attacker has white-box access only to this public backbone (architecture + gradients), not to the downstream classification head. Instead of the standard logit-based adversarial objective, the authors introduce a 'multi-modal guidance loss' that uses CLIP's text encoder to build source-aware semantic anchors (separate authentic/forgery text-prompt sets for 'Global Synthesis' vs. 'Local Editing' forgeries) and optimizes perturbations (via MI-FGSM plus a differentiable anti-aliased resampling step to bridge resolution gaps) to pull the adversarial image's CLIP embedding toward authentic-text anchors and away from forgery-text anchors, across the last two transformer blocks (S={N-1,N}).
threat_model: Attacker has white-box access (architecture and gradients) to the shared, publicly available upstream backbone (CLIP ViT-L/14 or OpenCLIP ConvNeXt) but treats the actual downstream detector's classification head D_theta as a complete black box (no parameter/gradient access). The attacker also assumes knowledge of the generative source category of the image being laundered (global synthesis vs. local editing) — described as realistic because 'the forgery creator typically knows how the content was generated.' The perturbation budget is bounded in L-infinity norm (epsilon in {4/255, 8/255}), and the goal is to flip the detector's verdict from Fake to Real while, as a further effect, causing multimodal explainable-forensics models (SIDA, FakeVLM) to generate fabricated textual justifications consistent with the false 'Real' verdict.
limitations: The paper's own scope restrictions: (1) the threat model assumes the attacker knows the generative-source category (global synthesis vs. local editing) of the target image to select the correct anchor set — this prior-knowledge assumption is acknowledged but only lightly justified ('the forgery creator typically knows how the content was generated'); it is not tested how the attack degrades if this label is wrong or unavailable, only that mismatched anchors are less effective. (2) All six victim detectors share a CLIP or OpenCLIP ViT-L/14-family backbone (per their own Table 4, 5 of 6 use variants of the same ViT-L/14 architecture) — the paper's premise depends on this specific de facto backbone monoculture and the results may not generalize to detectors with heterogeneous or non-CLIP backbones. (3) AIDE and FakeVLM were excluded entirely from the JPEG/blur robustness evaluation because of baseline instability on distorted inputs, so robustness claims are not established for those two models. (4) The Effort model shows an unexplained decline in attack effectiveness under severe blur (sigma>=2.0), which the authors attribute to the victim's own baseline degradation on blurred clean images rather than attack failure, but this is argued rather than independently isolated. (5) No comparison against prior anti-forensics/adversarial-transfer baselines from the anti-forensics literature (e.g., StealthDiffusion, frequency-based, or shallow-reconstruction methods cited in Related Work) is run empirically — the ablations compare only against the paper's own Untargeted/Coarse-grained variants, not against these external competing methods. (6) No defense or countermeasure is proposed or evaluated; the paper is purely an attack/vulnerability-disclosure paper.

datasets_used: SID-Set (Full-Synthesis and Tampered subsets, 20,000 each + 20,000 Authentic, 60,000 total) — used for SIDA
AIGCDetectBenchmark (14 generator subsets, 152,598 images total) — used for AIDE
FakeClue (5,000 images across Chameleon, Doc, FaceForensics++, GenImage-Agg, Satellite subsets) — used for FakeVLM
UniversalFakeDetect / UFD (100,329 images spanning GANs, Diffusion/Text-to-Image, Face & Manipulation, Natural Images) — used for LEGION and cross-generator generalization
Protocol-1 Cross-Dataset (aggregation of 7 Deepfake face datasets: CDF, DFDCP, WDF, DFR, DFDC, DFD, FFIW; 125,960 images total) — used for Effort and Forensics Adapter
ProGAN dataset — used to fine-tune LEGION's classification head per official protocol, and as the source for the t-SNE Dog/Cat visualization
quantitative_results: LEGION on UFD: Fake accuracy 74.7% (clean) -> 0.5% (epsilon=8/255), R-delta -99.4%
Forensics Adapter on Protocol-1: Fake accuracy 62.5% (clean) -> 5.6% (epsilon=8/255), R-delta -91.0%
Effort on Protocol-1: Fake accuracy 90.8% (clean) -> 9.4% (epsilon=8/255), R-delta -89.7%; Real accuracy 67.2% (clean) -> 95.5% (epsilon=8/255), R-delta +42.1%
AIDE on AIGCDetectBenchmark: Fake accuracy 96.5% (clean) -> 14.2% (epsilon=8/255), R-delta -85.2%
FakeVLM on FakeClue: Fake accuracy 99.3% (clean) -> 37.6% (epsilon=8/255), R-delta -62.2%
SIDA on SID-Set Tampered: Fake accuracy 93.7% (clean) -> 10.0% (epsilon=8/255), R-delta -89.3%; -87.1% at epsilon=4/255
SIDA on SID-Set Full-Synthesis: Fake accuracy 99.5% (clean) -> 26.5% (epsilon=8/255), R-delta -73.0%
Cross-generator generalization (LEGION/UFD): Diffusion-model subset avg accuracy 81.9% (clean) -> 0.25% (adversarial); GAN subset avg accuracy 91.6% (clean) -> 0.76% (adversarial)
t-SNE case study (ProGAN Dog/Cat subsets, LEGION): Fake detection accuracy collapses from 100.0% to 1.5% (Dog) and 0.0% (Cat); Real accuracy remains 100.0% post-attack
Ablation on LEGION Fake accuracy: Baseline 74.7%, Untargeted Disruption 34.5%, Coarse-grained Guidance 3.5%, ForgeryEraser 0.5%
Ablation on SIDA Fake accuracy: Baseline 93.7%, Untargeted 62.6%, Coarse-grained 25.0%, ForgeryEraser 10.0%
Ablation on FakeVLM Fake accuracy: Baseline 99.3%, Untargeted 80.7%, Coarse-grained 54.9%, ForgeryEraser 37.6%
Ablation on Effort Fake accuracy: Baseline 90.8%, Untargeted 91.4% (attack backfires, increasing detection), Coarse-grained 12.9%, ForgeryEraser 9.4%
baselines_compared: Untargeted Disruption (maximize feature divergence from input without directional text target) -- the paper's own ablation baseline, not a prior published method

Coarse-grained Guidance (multi-modal guidance loss using generic class-label text prompts, e.g. 'a real photo', instead of fine-grained source-aware attribute prompts) -- the paper's own ablation baseline
Mismatched-anchor variants (Global Synthesis anchors applied to Local Editing targets and vice versa) used to validate the source-aware design, not an external baseline
No external prior anti-forensics attack methods (e.g., StealthDiffusion, FakePolisher, frequency-based attacks cited in Related Work) were run as empirical comparison baselines in the experiments
